map_story_link.text: See on Map — Paul's Journey to Rome

sections.header: Verses 1—26 — Voyage to Rome; The Storm at Sea

heb.paragraph: the technical nautical term for the dreaded northeast Mediterranean storm (v.14). Ancient sailors knew this storm system by name: it blew from the northeast with hurricane force, making steering impossible. Luke's use of the precise technical term is one of many details confirming his eyewitness presence.

Paul's identification in v.23 — 'the God whose I am and whom I serve.' The double claim of belonging and service is one of the most compact expressions of discipleship in the NT. Ownership precedes service; identity precedes activity.

places._raw_html:
Places

Fair Havens — Crete
34.9333° N, 24.9833° E
The small harbour on Crete's south coast where Paul warns against continuing (vv.9–10). The Day of Atonement (Yom Kippur) falling in late September/early October places the scene in the dangerous late sailing season. Paul's maritime warning is ignored — which makes his subsequent angelic assurance all the more dramatic.
The Mediterranean — Open Sea
35.0000° N, 14.5000° E
Acts 27 is one of antiquity's most detailed nautical narratives. The storm drives them from Crete toward Malta — roughly 900 km before the wind. Luke's precise technical vocabulary (frapping the hull, the sea anchor, soundings) confirms personal presence as an eyewitness.

tl.name: The Storm — Two Weeks Adrift
tl.text: Paul's voyage to Rome encounters a two-week Mediterranean storm that destroys the ship. An angel assures Paul that no lives will be lost — God has given him all 276 people on board. The ship's company survives because one man belongs to God and has an appointment in Rome.

tl.name: Winter at Malta; Voyage to Rome

refs.note: "You must stand trial before Caesar" — the Lord's promise that the angel echoes in v.24. The shipwreck is part of the route to Rome, not a setback to it.

The storm, the prophet on board, the endangered crew — the structural parallel with Jonah is intentional. Paul is the anti-Jonah: where Jonah fled his commission, Paul is fulfilling his; where Jonah endangered the ship, Paul's presence saves it.
"Some went out on the sea in ships... He stilled the storm... and he guided them to their desired haven" — the psalm of sea rescue that provides the theological framework.

notes.note: MacArthur on Paul's speech during the storm: he does not simply offer comfort but grounds it in specific divine revelation. 'An angel of the God whose I am and whom I serve' — MacArthur notes the order: belonging precedes serving. Paul's authority to speak for God in the storm comes from his identity as God's possession.

"God has graciously given you the lives of all who sail with you" — MacArthur: God gives the 275 other lives to Paul specifically. The entire ship is preserved because of one man's divine mission — an illustration of how God's purposes for one person can overflow into blessing all who share their circumstances.

Calvin on Paul's assurance during the storm: the contrast is deliberate — the crew threw cargo overboard in despair; the apostle delivers a divine promise with complete calm. Paul's calm is not temperament but faith: he has a specific promise and states it as settled fact. Faith-based courage is not the absence of fear but the presence of a better certainty.

Calvin on Paul's ignored advice: the one man on board with divine knowledge is the one whose judgment is disregarded. The word of God is routinely set aside in favour of the world's practical expertise — with predictable results.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes that "the Fast" refers to Yom Kippur (Day of Atonement) — the only day in the Jewish calendar designated as a fast. It falls in late September or October, establishing the timing precisely and confirming Luke's attention to calendar detail.

The NET notes that "they passed ropes under the ship" (hypozōnnyntes to ploion) is frapping — a maritime practice of wrapping the hull with ropes to prevent planks from splitting under storm stress. The technical detail confirms Luke's eyewitness knowledge.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: "Men, I can see that our voyage is going to be disastrous" — Robertson notes Paul says "I can see," not "the Spirit tells me" — this is practical judgment from an experienced traveller who had already been shipwrecked three times (2 Cor 11:25). The advice is ignored, making his subsequent angelic assurance both more dramatic and more credible.

"Not one of you will be lost; only the ship will be destroyed" — Robertson notes the precision: lives preserved, ship lost. God's promises are specific, not general. Robertson: "Paul states it as settled fact — this is faith, not optimism."

keener.source: Craig S. Keener, Acts: An Exegetical Commentary (4 vols., 2012–2015) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Keener notes the navigation significance of 'the Day of Atonement': Yom Kippur falls in September/October — the period when the ancients considered Mediterranean sailing dangerous (approximately September 14 to November 11 was increasing-risk season). Paul's warning was maritimely sound as well as spiritually grounded.

Keener provides background on the Eurakulos (Northeaster). Pliny the Elder and other ancient writers describe this storm system. The storm's route — taking the ship from Crete toward Malta — is consistent with actual Mediterranean storm patterns, confirming the voyage's historicity.

hist.context: Acts 27 is one of the most detailed eyewitness voyage accounts in ancient literature — compared favourably by classicists to the best Greek and Roman seafaring narratives. But Luke's purpose is theological: the ship full of condemned prisoners is preserved because one man on board belongs to God and has an appointment in Rome. The angel's assurance (v.24: 'God has graciously given you the lives of all who sail with you') means 275 other people are kept alive by virtue of their association with Paul.

sections.header: Verses 27—44 — Shipwreck at Malta: All Were Brought Safely to Land

heb.gloss: gave thanks to God in front of them all
heb.paragraph: Paul's bread-breaking in vv.35–36 — taking bread, giving thanks, breaking it — uses the same fourfold eucharistic action as the Last Supper (Luke 22:19) and Emmaus (Luke 24:30). The Gospel's table practice appears in the middle of a shipwreck, in front of 275 non-Christians.

v.44 — the fulfilment of the angel's promise (v.24). The passive 'were saved' (diesōthēsan) is Luke's theological signal: this was God's act. Every one of the 276 people on board survived — not one was lost.

places._raw_html:
Places

Malta
35.9375° N, 14.3754° E
The island of Melita (Malta) — identified by ancient sources and modern geography as the shipwreck location. St. Paul's Bay on Malta's northeastern coast is the traditional site. Archaeological and navigational studies confirm that the ship's drift from Crete is consistent with Malta as the landfall.
St. Paul's Bay — Malta
35.9500° N, 14.3950° E
The 'bay with a sandy beach' (v.39) where the ship ran aground. The description is consistent with Malta's northern coast. The centurion Julius — who protected Paul throughout the voyage (v.43) — here again uses his authority to ensure Paul's survival.

tl.name: Shipwreck at Malta — All 276 Saved
tl.text: The ship runs aground at Malta. All 276 reach land safely — the exact fulfilment of the angel's promise. The centurion protects Paul from execution. The eucharistic bread-breaking before dawn is the spiritual climax of the voyage narrative.

refs.note: "He took bread, gave thanks and broke it" — the Last Supper action that Paul replicates in vv.35–36.
"He took bread, gave thanks, broke it and began to give it to them. Then their eyes were opened" — Luke consistently uses the eucharistic action as the moment of spiritual recognition.
"God has graciously given you the lives of all who sail with you" — the promise fulfilled exactly in v.44: "everyone reached land safely."

notes.note: MacArthur on Paul's bread-breaking: the eucharistic action in front of 275 non-Christians is a public act of faith. Paul visibly demonstrates that he trusts God by eating, inviting others to follow. The crew follows his lead (v.36). Faith is contagious when visibly demonstrated.

MacArthur on Julius's protection: Julius's repeated protection of Paul (v.3; v.43) is the human instrument of divine preservation. God's providential protection regularly operates through the actions of non-Christians who have no awareness of their role in his plan.

"Not one of you will lose a single hair from his head" — Calvin: the divine promise is as specific as humanly possible. "Not a hair" echoes Luke 21:18. God's promises are not vague reassurances but specific commitments — and their fulfilment is equally specific.

Calvin on 'everyone reached land safely': the narrative proof of Paul's faith (v.25: 'I have faith in God that it will happen just as he told me'). Not faith that avoids danger but faith that walks through danger holding specific divine promises.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes the debate about whether this is a formal Eucharist. The language parallels the Last Supper (Luke 22:19) and Emmaus meal (Luke 24:30). The NET suggests that while this may not be the formal sacrament, Luke deliberately uses eucharistic language — the meal carries the theology of the Lord's Table even if it is not the formal sacrament.
The NET notes the textual variant: most manuscripts read 276; a few read 76. The NET follows the majority reading of 276 as the harder reading — more likely to be accidentally reduced than inflated.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: "He took some bread and gave thanks to God in front of them all. Then he broke it" — Robertson notes the deliberate eucharistic language: the four verbs (took, gave thanks, broke, began to eat) are precisely the Last Supper verbs. Paul acts as the host of a meal of thanksgiving in the middle of a crisis — a visible demonstration of faith to 275 unbelievers.

"Everyone reached land safely" — Robertson on hapas (all, everyone) completing the promise of v.24. The angel's specific promise has been kept exactly. God's specific promises are specifically fulfilled.

keener.source: Craig S. Keener, Acts: An Exegetical Commentary (4 vols., 2012–2015) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Keener notes the navigational precision: the soundings of 120 feet, then 90 feet are consistent with the approach to Malta's northern coast. Modern hydrographic surveys of St. Paul's Bay confirm the depth contours match Luke's account exactly — powerful evidence of eyewitness accuracy.

Keener notes that 276 people on a grain ship is historically plausible. Josephus (Life 3.15) mentions a grain ship with 600 on board. The Alexandria-to-Rome grain route routinely carried large numbers; 276 is not exaggerated.

hist.context: The shipwreck narrative reaches its climax in Paul's eucharistic bread-breaking (vv.33–36) and the grounding that fulfils the angel's promise exactly (vv.41–44). The bread-breaking in front of all 276 people is the Gospel's table in the midst of disaster — the community of faith sharing food with those who do not yet share their faith. Every one of the 276 reaches land safely: the angel's specific promise is specifically kept.

ppl.name: Paul↗ People
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVMuch time had been lost, and sailing had already become dangerous because by now it was after the Day of Atonement. So Paul warned them,ESVSince much time had passed, and the voyage was now dangerous because even the Fast was already over, Paul advised them,NIVThe next day we landed at Sidon; and Julius, in kindness to Paul, allowed him to go to his friends so they could provide for his needs.ESVThe next day we put in at Sidon. And Julius treated Paul kindly and gave him leave to go to his friends and be cared for.

src.title: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews (c.93 AD)
src.quote: Josephus provides extensive first-century background for the political, religious, and social conditions described in Acts — the Herodian family, Pharisees and Sadducees, Roman provincial governance.
src.note: Acts’ historical details consistently align with Josephus’s independent account of the same period, supporting the narrative’s historical credibility.

src.title: Dead Sea Scrolls (1QS, 1QM)
src.quote: The Qumran community texts illuminate the diversity of Second Temple Judaism from which Christianity emerged — their eschatological expectations, community rules, and temple criticism parallel aspects of the early church.
src.note: The Scrolls demonstrate that the early church’s practices (community meals, shared goods, eschatological expectation) were not unprecedented within Jewish renewal movements.

src.title: Greco-Roman Travel Literature and Epistolary Conventions
src.quote: Acts’ travel narrative (ch.13–28) conforms to the conventions of ancient Greek travel literature, and Paul’s speeches follow rhetorical models documented in Greco-Roman oratory.
src.note: The Hellenistic literary conventions confirm Acts’ composition for a sophisticated Greco-Roman audience and support its historical reliability as ancient historiography.

rec.who: John Chrysostom, Homilies on Acts (c.400 AD)
rec.text: "Chrysostom’s 55 homilies on Acts are the most thorough patristic commentary, emphasising the Spirit’s active role and the apostles’ moral example for the church."

tx.title: Western text (Codex Bezae D) vs Alexandrian text
tx.content: Alexandrian(P45, P74, א, B) is the primary scholarly text.Western(Codex Bezae D) is 10–15% longer, with additions that appear to expand and harmonise.
tx.note: Acts has the most significant NT textual variation. The Western text of Acts is not simply corrupt but may preserve early oral expansions. Most scholars follow the shorter Alexandrian text as more original.

tx.title: P45 and early papyrus witnesses
tx.content: The Chester Beatty Papyrus (P45, c.250 AD) is the earliest substantial Acts manuscript, generally supporting the Alexandrian text with some unique readings.
tx.note: The papyrus evidence has largely confirmed the Alexandrian tradition as the best text of Acts, though the Western text’s substantial additions remain a subject of scholarly investigation.

debate.title: Authorship and Historicity of Scripture Walkthrough

positions.name: Traditional Lukan authorship (Hengel, Keener, classical scholarship)
positions.proponents: Attributes Acts to Luke the physician (Col 4:14), a companion of Paul, on the basis of the "we" passages, historical accuracy, and early church tradition.
positions.argument: Luke’s medical vocabulary (noted by Hobart) and the detailed geographical and political accuracy of Acts support the traditional attribution.

positions.name: Anonymous/secondary authorship (Haenchen, Conzelmann)
positions.proponents: Questions Lukan authorship on the basis of discrepancies between Acts’ Paul and the Pauline letters, suggesting a later author using Lukan sources.
positions.argument: This view notes that Acts does not always match Paul’s letters precisely, and argues the "we" passages may be a literary device rather than eyewitness markers.
